name.text: What was the primary factor contributing to systemic discrimination and socioeco...

questiontext.text: What was the primary factor contributing to systemic discrimination and socioeconomic disparities for Indigenous populations in Canada?

answer.text: The introduction of universal healthcare
The Indian Act and residential schools
The expansion of the welfare state
The rise of industrialization

name.text: According to the OECD, how much more does the richest 10% earn compared to the p...

questiontext.text: According to the OECD, how much more does the richest 10% earn compared to the poorest 10% in its member countries?

answer.text: 5.2 times
7.8 times
9.6 times
12.3 times

name.text: What was a key characteristic of income inequality in Canada between 1946 and 19...

questiontext.text: What was a key characteristic of income inequality in Canada between 1946 and 1981?

answer.text: It fluctuated significantly due to market volatility
It remained relatively stable despite economic transformations
It declined steadily due to redistributive policies
It increased at an accelerating rate

name.text: What is the main critique of the "Income Inequality Hypothesis" regarding health...

questiontext.text: What is the main critique of the "Income Inequality Hypothesis" regarding health outcomes?

answer.text: It fails to consider absolute income levels
It overemphasizes social spending policies
It assumes health disparities are unrelated to income
It ignores the role of economic mobility

name.text: What is a significant factor contributing to widening wealth inequality in Canad...

questiontext.text: What is a significant factor contributing to widening wealth inequality in Canada?

answer.text: Increased social spending on education
Growth in financial and real estate assets among wealthier groups
Declining corporate tax rates
A rise in manufacturing jobs

name.text: What is one key factor explaining lower educational attainment among Indigenous ...

questiontext.text: What is one key factor explaining lower educational attainment among Indigenous communities in Canada?

answer.text: A lack of available financial aid
Systemic barriers and historical discrimination
A lower emphasis on higher education within these communities
An overall decline in post-secondary enrollment rates

name.text: A labor force of 100,000 is divided into 20% high-skilled, 50% middle-skilled, a...

questiontext.text: A labor force of 100,000 is divided into 20% high-skilled, 50% middle-skilled, and 30% low-skilled workers. Demand changes as follows: o High-skilled: +15% o Middle-skilled: -25% o Low-skilled: 0% What is the total net change in workforce size?

name.text: The Market Basket Measure (MBM) defines poverty based on:

questiontext.text: The Market Basket Measure (MBM) defines poverty based on:

answer.text: The share of national income going to the bottom 10%
The cost of a specific basket of basic goods and services
The percentage of people earning below half of the mean income
The minimum wage set by the government

name.text: When comparing inequality across countries using the Gini coefficient, it is cru...

questiontext.text: When comparing inequality across countries using the Gini coefficient, it is crucial to:

answer.text: Ensure that data sources and income definitions are consistent
Use only pre-tax income for a fair comparison
Ignore country-specific economic structures
Assume all countries have the same population size

name.text: If the Gini coefficient of a country is 0.8, it suggests that:

questiontext.text: If the Gini coefficient of a country is 0.8, it suggests that:

answer.text: Income is equally distributed
Almost all income is concentrated in a few individuals
The bottom 50% of the population earns at least 50% of total income
The country has no poverty

name.text: The evolution of the Gini coefficient in Canada suggests:

questiontext.text: The evolution of the Gini coefficient in Canada suggests:

answer.text: It has been consistently decreasing since 1970
It increased in the 1990s and then started declining in the 2000s
It remained constant across all decades
The richest quintile lost income share over time

name.text: If all income gains in an economy go to the top 10%, the Gini coefficient will:

questiontext.text: If all income gains in an economy go to the top 10%, the Gini coefficient will:

answer.text: Decrease
Remain unchanged
Increase
Approach zero

name.text: Which of the following is an empirical observation about inequality and growth?

questiontext.text: Which of the following is an empirical observation about inequality and growth?

answer.text: All high-income countries have low inequality
There is no clear relationship between GDP growth and inequality
Economic growth always leads to lower inequality
Inequality is highest in countries with no economic growth

name.text: The relationship between poverty and inequality in empirical studies suggests:

questiontext.text: The relationship between poverty and inequality in empirical studies suggests:

answer.text: There is no necessary link between poverty and inequality
All high-inequality countries have high poverty
A lower Gini coefficient always eliminates poverty
Poverty levels are unaffected by inequality

name.text: A decreasing poverty rate with a stable Gini coefficient suggests that:

questiontext.text: A decreasing poverty rate with a stable Gini coefficient suggests that:

answer.text: Inequality has worsened
Income growth has benefited the poor without changing relative inequality
The middle class is shrinking
The bottom 10% lost income share
